source_paper_title: Can Learning Be Explained by Local Optimality in Robust Low-Rank Matrix Recovery?
context:
Page 12, Section 2.3 (Asymmetric matrix completion), discussion immediately after Theorem 6

We note that this result does not refute the possibility of existing highly rank-imbalanced solutions—in particular, those with \(\mathrm{rank}(W_1^{\star})+\mathrm{rank}(W_2^{\star})\ge k\)—as local optima. Although our result does not refute this possibility, we conjecture that highly rank-imbalanced solutions are unlikely to emerge as local optima. We leave a rigorous proof of this conjecture for future work.
title: Nonexistence of local minima among highly rank-imbalanced true solutions in noisy asymmetric matrix completion with \(\ell_1\) loss
latex:
Let integers \(d_1,d_2\ge 1\), a target rank \(r\le \min\{d_1,d_2\}\), and a search rank \(k>2r\) be given. Let \(X^{\star}\in\mathbb{R}^{d_1\times d_2}\) be any rank-\(r\) matrix. Consider a measurement set \(\Omega\subseteq[d_1]\times[d_2]\) (possibly \(\Omega=[d_1]\times[d_2]\)) and an observed matrix \(Y\in\mathbb{R}^{d_1\times d_2}\) such that
\[
Y[\alpha,\beta]=X^{\star}[\alpha,\beta]+E[\alpha,\beta]\quad\text{for all }(\alpha,\beta)\in\Omega,
\]
where \(E\in\mathbb{R}^{d_1\times d_2}\) is a noise matrix supported on \(\Omega\) and at least a constant fraction of observed entries are corrupted by nontrivial noise (e.g., there exist constants \(t_0>0\) and \(p_0\in(0,1]\) such that for each \((\alpha,\beta)\in\Omega\), independently, \(|E[\alpha,\beta]|\ge t_0\) with probability at least \(p_0\)). Define the nonsmooth Burer--Monteiro objective (asymmetric matrix completion with \(\ell_1\)-loss)
\[
 f(W_1,W_2)=\frac{1}{|\Omega|}\sum_{(\alpha,\beta)\in\Omega}\bigl|Y[\alpha,\beta]-(W_1W_2)[\alpha,\beta]\bigr|,
\]
where the decision variables are \(W_1\in\mathbb{R}^{d_1\times k}\) and \(W_2\in\mathbb{R}^{k\times d_2}\). Let the set of true solutions be
\[
\mathcal{W}=\{(W_1,W_2): W_1W_2=X^{\star}\}.
\]
Call a true solution \((W_1,W_2)\in\mathcal{W}\) highly rank-imbalanced if \(\mathrm{rank}(W_1)+\mathrm{rank}(W_2)\ge k\). (Equivalently, it is not in \(\mathcal{W}(k-1):=\{(W_1,W_2)\in\mathcal{W}:\mathrm{rank}(W_1)+\mathrm{rank}(W_2)\le k-1\}\).)

Problem: Prove (or disprove) that, under the above noisy asymmetric matrix completion model (including the fully observed case \(\Omega=[d_1]\times[d_2]\)), no highly rank-imbalanced true solution is a local minimizer of \(f\). Formally, establish conditions on \((d_1,d_2,r,k,\Omega,E)\) under which
\[
\forall (W_1,W_2)\in\mathcal{W}\text{ with }\mathrm{rank}(W_1)+\mathrm{rank}(W_2)\ge k,\quad (W_1,W_2)\notin \operatorname{LocMin}(f),
\]
where \(\operatorname{LocMin}(f)\) denotes the set of local minimizers of \(f\) over \(\mathbb{R}^{d_1\times k}\times\mathbb{R}^{k\times d_2}\).
